title: Tarte flambee
subtitle: Alsatian thin-crust tart with creme fraiche, onion and lardons
excerpt: Tarte flambee is a thin-crust Alsatian tart on a yeasted bread base so thin it crisps entirely through. The topping is creme fraiche seasoned with nutmeg, very thinly sliced onion and smoked lardons. It bakes at the highest oven temperature for about 10 minutes on a preheated tray. The edges char slightly, which is correct and desirable. The whole process takes about 1 hour 30 minutes including the dough rest.
sourceNotes: Tarte flambee originates in Alsace, where it was traditionally baked in a wood-fired bread oven at the beginning of the firing to test the temperature. It is documented in Paul Bocuse's 'La Cuisine du Marche' (1976) and in Elizabeth David's 'French Provincial Cooking' (1960). The Alsatian association is consistent across all primary sources; the dish crossed into German cuisine as Flammkuchen.

recipe.yieldDescription: 2 tarts, each about 30 x 20 cm, serves 4
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made up to 2 hours ahead and left at room temperature. Assemble and bake just before serving as the base softens quickly once topped.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven at maximum heat; preheat the baking tray in the oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread dough rolled or stretched to 2 to 3 mm thick, much thinner than a pizza base. At this thickness the dough crisps entirely through during baking rather than having a doughy interior, and the edges char slightly at the maximum oven temperature. The charring is characteristic and desirable, not a mistake: it provides a bitter contrast to the rich, creamy topping. The base needs to be on a hot tray in a very hot oven; a cold tray produces a pale, soft result.

content.text: The characteristic quality of a good tarte flambee is its

. Roll or stretch the dough as thin as you can without tearing, no more than 3 mm. The oven must be at its maximum heat, and the baking tray must be preheated inside it: loading a cold tray is the most common reason for a pale, soft result.

Season the creme fraiche mixture with nutmeg, salt and white pepper only. Do not add garlic, herbs or anything else: the simplicity of the topping is what defines the dish. The lardons are optional for a vegetarian version; replace with thinly sliced mushrooms or caramelised onion and the dish stays true to its character.

Combine the 200 g flour, 2 g yeast and 4 g salt. Add the 120 ml tepid water and 10 ml olive oil and mix to a smooth, firm dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Cover and leave at room temperature for 45 minutes.

Heat the oven to its maximum temperature (250 degrees C fan or as high as it goes) with a heavy baking tray inside. Mix the 150 g creme fraiche with the 50 g soured cream, a pinch of nutmeg and a little salt and white pepper.

Divide the dough into two. On a floured surface, roll each piece to a thin rectangle, 2 to 3 mm thick and roughly 30 x 20 cm. Spread half the creme fraiche mixture over each base, leaving a 1 cm border. Scatter over the thinly sliced onion and lardons if using. Carefully slide the tart onto the hot baking tray. Bake for 8 to 12 minutes until the edges are charred and blistered, the creme fraiche is bubbling, and the base is crisp all the way through when lifted. Serve immediately.

items.problem: Base is pale, soft and doughy underneath
items.solution: The baking tray was not hot enough. It must be preheated in the oven for at least 20 minutes before the tart goes on. Sliding the tart onto a cold tray means the base steams rather than crisps.

items.problem: Creme fraiche separated and looks watery
items.solution: The topping was spread too thick, trapping steam, or the oven temperature was too low. Use a thin layer of the creme fraiche mixture, no more than 3 mm deep. A higher temperature drives off moisture faster and prevents separation.

items.problem: Dough tears when rolling this thin
items.solution: The gluten needed more relaxation time. After the initial roll, cover the dough for 5 minutes to let it relax, then continue stretching it. The 45-minute rest in the bowl helps but the dough may still resist; be patient rather than forcing it.
